metadata.summary: Prompt testing, quality metrics, evaluation rubrics, A/B comparison, debugging workflows, and deployment checklists for production LLM prompts. Covers LLM-assisted prompt analysis and optimization.

content.title: Prompt Engineering — Evaluation and Debugging
content.summary: Prompts without a test suite degrade silently. This methodology covers pre-flight checklists, test case design, quality metrics, evaluation rubrics, A/B comparison techniques, debugging workflows for incorrect or inconsistent outputs, and the full deployment checklist for production prompts. Includes LLM-assisted prompt analysis prompts.

why.p: Prompt quality cannot be assessed by reading the prompt alone — it must be measured against diverse inputs including edge cases and adversarial inputs. Without a defined baseline and threshold, teams ship prompts that work on 3 happy-path examples and fail on real traffic. Systematic evaluation (test cases, metrics, rubrics) catches issues before production and provides a regression baseline after changes.

list.item: Before deploying any prompt to production — all prompts need a minimum test coverage.
After modifying an existing prompt — regression testing prevents new failures.
When outputs are inconsistent across runs — debugging workflow identifies root cause.
When comparing prompt variants — A/B framework gives a structured decision.
When quality degrades in production — evaluation metrics quantify the regression.
One-off exploratory prompts — full evaluation overhead is not justified.
Prompts used only for synthetic data generation where output correctness is manually reviewed.

section.name: pre-flight-checklist
section.p: Before writing any prompt, verify these requirements:

checklist.check: Clear objective defined — what should the model accomplish?
Success criteria defined — how will success be measured?
Input format documented — what data will be provided?
Output format documented — what structure is required?
Edge cases identified — what unusual inputs are expected?
Constraints listed — length limits, style requirements, safety rules?
Capability match confirmed — can the model handle this task?
Context window budget calculated — total tokens within limits?
Input sanitization applied — user inputs cleaned and validated?
Sensitive data handling — PII, credentials filtered?

section.name: prompt-writing-checklist
section.p: Use during prompt writing to verify structural quality:

checklist.check: Role definition present in system prompt.
Task description is explicit and unambiguous.
Context section is clearly marked.
Output format (format, length, style) is defined.
Examples included where needed.
Delimiters (XML tags or markers) separate sections.
No ambiguity — instructions have single interpretation.
Specific language used — no vague terms ("good", "better", "nice").
Edge cases addressed — instructions for unusual inputs.
Error handling defined — what to do when task cannot be completed.
Constraints stated — what NOT to do is clear.
Prompt is concise — no unnecessary words or redundant instructions.

section.name: quality-metrics
section.p: Standard quality metrics by task type:

list.item: Task completion — Did output achieve goal? Use for all tasks.
Accuracy — Correctness of information. Use for factual tasks.
Relevance — Output matches request. Use for open-ended tasks.
Format compliance — Follows specified format. Use for structured output.
Consistency — Same input yields similar output. Use for production systems.
Latency — Response time. Use for real-time applications.
Token efficiency — Tokens used per output. Use for cost optimization.

rule.statement: Every production prompt MUST have: a defined accuracy or quality baseline, a minimum acceptable threshold, and a regression test set of at least 10 representative inputs. Consistency must be measured across at least 3 independent runs on the same inputs.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline, teams cannot distinguish improvements from regressions. Without a threshold, prompts are deployed on subjective judgment. Without regression tests, every change risks breaking existing functionality.

section.name: test-coverage
section.p: Test case categories for comprehensive prompt coverage:

checklist.check: Happy path — Standard inputs work correctly.
Edge cases — Unusual inputs are handled gracefully.
Error cases — Invalid inputs produce appropriate error responses.
Adversarial inputs — Injection attempts are blocked or handled safely.
Empty/null inputs — Missing data is handled without hallucination.
Maximum-length inputs — Context overflow handled gracefully.

example.title: Test case documentation template
example.code:
## Test Case Template
**ID:** TC-001 **Description:** [What this tests] **Input:** [Test input] **Expected Output:** [What should happen] **Actual Output:** [What happened] **Result:** PASS/FAIL **Notes:** [Any observations]

example.title: LLM-assisted test case generation

section.name: evaluation-rubrics
section.p: Rubric-based evaluation enables reproducible, scored assessment of prompt outputs.

example.title: Response evaluation prompt
example.code:
Evaluate the following AI response based on the given criteria.
Original prompt: <prompt> {ORIGINAL_PROMPT} </prompt>
AI response: <response> {AI_RESPONSE} </response>
Evaluate on these criteria (1-5 scale):
1. **Accuracy**: Is the information correct? Score: [1-5] Reasoning: [explanation]
2. **Relevance**: Does it address the prompt? Score: [1-5] Reasoning: [explanation]
3. **Completeness**: Are all aspects covered? Score: [1-5] Reasoning: [explanation]
4. **Clarity**: Is it easy to understand? Score: [1-5] Reasoning: [explanation]
5. **Helpfulness**: Is it useful to the user? Score: [1-5] Reasoning: [explanation]
Overall score: [average] Key strengths: [list] Areas for improvement: [list]

example.title: LLM-assisted rubric generation
example.code:
Create an evaluation rubric for assessing outputs from this prompt.
Prompt: <prompt> {PROMPT} </prompt>
Use case: {USE_CASE}
Create a rubric with: 1. 4-6 evaluation criteria 2. 1-5 scale for each criterion 3. Clear descriptions for each score level 4. Relative importance weights
Format: ## Criterion: [Name] Weight: [X%] Description: [What this measures]
| Score | Description | |-------|-------------| | 5 | [Exceptional - description] | | 4 | [Good - description] | | 3 | [Acceptable - description] | | 2 | [Below average - description] | | 1 | [Poor - description] |

example.title: A/B prompt comparison template
example.code:
Compare these two responses and determine which is better.
Prompt: <prompt> {ORIGINAL_PROMPT} </prompt>
Response A: <response_a> {RESPONSE_A} </response_a>
Response B: <response_b> {RESPONSE_B} </response_b>
Compare on: 1. Accuracy 2. Completeness 3. Clarity 4. Helpfulness
For each criterion, indicate which response is better (A, B, or Tie) and why.
Final verdict: - Winner: [A/B/Tie] - Confidence: [high/medium/low] - Key differentiator: [main reason for the decision]

example.title: LLM-assisted prompt version comparison
example.code:
Compare these prompt versions and recommend the better one.
Version A: <version_a> {PROMPT_VERSION_A} </version_a>
Version B: <version_b> {PROMPT_VERSION_B} </version_b>
Use case: {USE_CASE}
Compare on: 1. Clarity of instructions 2. Output quality (estimated) 3. Token efficiency 4. Edge case handling 5. Consistency (estimated)
Provide: - Side-by-side comparison table - Strengths/weaknesses of each - Recommendation with reasoning - Suggested hybrid version if applicable

section.name: debugging-workflows
section.p: Structured debugging for the three most common failure modes:

example.title: Debug incorrect outputs
example.code:
Help me debug this prompt that produces incorrect outputs.
Prompt: <prompt> {PROMPT} </prompt>
Expected output: <expected> {EXPECTED_OUTPUT} </expected>
Actual output (example): <actual> {ACTUAL_OUTPUT} </actual>
Analyze: 1. What's the discrepancy? 2. Why might the model produce this output? 3. What in the prompt is causing this?
Provide: - Root cause analysis - Specific fix recommendations - Fixed prompt version - Test cases to verify fix

example.title: Fix inconsistent outputs
Reduce hallucinations
example.code:
This prompt causes the model to hallucinate information. Help me ground it.
Prompt: <prompt> {PROMPT} </prompt>
Context/Source (if any): <context> {CONTEXT} </context>
Example hallucination: {HALLUCINATION_EXAMPLE}
Modify the prompt to: 1. Anchor responses to provided context 2. Encourage "I don't know" responses 3. Require source citations 4. Prevent fabrication
Provide: - Grounded prompt version - Specific anti-hallucination instructions - Validation approach

section.name: prompt-analysis-prompts
section.p: LLM prompts for analyzing and improving existing prompts:

example.title: Comprehensive prompt quality analysis
example.code:
Analyze the following prompt for effectiveness and suggest improvements.
Prompt to analyze: <prompt> {PROMPT_TO_ANALYZE} </prompt>
Evaluate these aspects:
1. **Clarity** - Are instructions unambiguous? - Is the task clearly defined?
2. **Specificity** - Is the output format specified? - Are constraints explicit?
3. **Completeness** - Are edge cases addressed? - Is error handling defined?
4. **Efficiency** - Are there unnecessary tokens? - Could it be more concise?
5. **Security** - Is user input safely handled? - Are there injection vulnerabilities?
Provide: - Score for each aspect (1-5) - Specific issues found - Concrete improvement suggestions - Rewritten improved version

example.title: Simplify complex prompt
example.code:
Simplify the following prompt while maintaining its effectiveness.
Original prompt: <prompt> {COMPLEX_PROMPT} </prompt>
Goals: - Reduce token count by at least 30% - Maintain same output quality - Keep essential instructions - Remove redundancy
Provide: 1. Simplified prompt 2. Token count comparison (estimate) 3. What was removed and why 4. Any potential quality trade-offs

example.title: Evaluate prompt security
example.code:
Evaluate this prompt for security vulnerabilities.
Prompt: <prompt> {PROMPT} </prompt>
System context: {SYSTEM_CONTEXT}
Check for: 1. Prompt injection vulnerabilities 2. Jailbreak susceptibility 3. Data leakage risks 4. Privilege escalation risks 5. Output manipulation risks
For each vulnerability found: - Severity: High/Medium/Low - Attack vector: How it could be exploited - Mitigation: How to fix it
Provide: - Security assessment summary - Specific vulnerabilities found - Hardened prompt version - Testing recommendations

checklist.check: All tests passing — no known failures on the test set.
Performance acceptable — latency within defined limits.
Cost acceptable — token usage within budget.
Security review complete — injection vectors addressed.
Documentation complete — prompt purpose and usage documented.
Metrics collection configured — latency, errors, costs.
Alerting configured — notify on quality degradation or errors.
Logging enabled — audit trail available.
Rollback plan documented — how to revert if needed.
Smoke tests pass in production after deploy.
Initial traffic monitored — watch for issues in first 24h.

how-to.step: Identify bottleneck — collect specific failing cases with actual vs. expected output.
Analyze failure patterns — categorize: wrong format, hallucination, missed edge case, inconsistency, off-topic.
Prioritize fixes — address highest-frequency failure mode first.
Apply one change at a time — add examples, add constraints, add CoT, or simplify task.
Re-run the full test set — verify improvement and check for regressions.
Measure impact — quantify improvement on the metric that mattered.
Document changes — record what was changed, why, and what the measured impact was.

list.item: promptfoo — CLI for evaluating and comparing prompts against test cases. Install: npm install -g promptfoo. Docs: promptfoo.dev.
llm (Simon Willison) — CLI for querying multiple LLMs, useful for A/B testing prompts. Install: pip install llm. Docs: github.com/simonw/llm.
DeepEval — OSS. Pytest-style LLM evaluation with many metrics. Agent-friendly.
LangSmith — SaaS. Prompt versioning, tracing, dataset-based eval. Agent-friendly.
Braintrust — SaaS. Prompt experiments, scoring, datasets via API. Agent-friendly.
Langfuse — OSS/SaaS. Prompt management and tracing; self-hostable. Agent-friendly.
PromptLayer — SaaS. Prompt version control and analytics.
